coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots modified polymetallic oxyphosphide hollow nanoboxes for boosting supercapacitor applications
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

Liu, Yu
coredata.dc:description: The regulation of composition and structural design of electrods are pivotal in advancing high-performance supercapacitors. In this study, we prepare a Fe-doped NiCo oxyphosphide (Fe-NiCoOP) nanobox with a hollow structure, which is further modified with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQD). This innovative design integrates multiple high-performance elements, leading to a substantial enhancement in electrochemical properties. The introduction of phosphorus (P) substantially improves the material’s conductivity, optimizes its electrochemical active sites, and increases cycle stability. The integration of NGQD forms a distinctive heterostructure with Fe-NiCoOP, which not only refines the charge transport pathways but also markedly accelerates the reaction kinetics of the electrode material. Furthermore, the hollow architecture of the nanocages provides extensive permeation and storage spaces for the electrolyte, while also substantially increasing the redox reaction sites, thereby elevating the overall electrochemical performance. The synergistic combination of these advantages results in the Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD electrode material exhibiting an exceptional specific capacity of 1136.5 F g−1. When utilized in supercapacitors, this material demonstrates a remarkable energy density of 78.7 Wh kg−1. Notably, the device retains 81.6% of its initial capacitance after 8000 charge–discharge cycles, underscoring its outstanding cyclic stability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Supercapacitor
Polymetallic oxyphosphide
Nanoboxes
Energy density
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the significant science and technology projects of LongMen Laboratory in Henan Province (231100220100), the significant science and technology projects of LongMen Laboratory in Henan Province (231100221100), the Key research and development program in Natural Science Foundation of Henan (231111222200), the College Students Innovations Special Project (Grant Nos. 202510482012), the Key Scientific Research Projects of Higher Education Institutions in Henan Province (Grant No. 25A140012) and Nanjing/Jiangsu XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd. of China.

Ltd. of China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic illustration of the formation process of the Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD.

(a) The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of both Fe-NiCoOP and Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD. (b) The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey spectra, (c) to (i) are the detailed XPS spectra for the Fe 2p, Ni 2p, Co 2p, P 2p, O 1s, C 1s, and N 1s orbitals, respectively, for both Fe-NiCoOP and Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD.
SEM images of (a) FeNiCo-PBA cubes and (b) Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD cages. (c–e) TEM and HRTEM images of Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD cages. (f–m) TEM and EDS elemental mapping images of the Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD nanocage.

(a) and (b) Present a comparative analysis of CV and GCD curves for FeNiCo-PBA, Fe-NiCoOP, and Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD. (c) and (d) Display the full CV and GCD profiles of Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD, respectively. (e) Showcases the specific capacitance values and Nyquist plots for these three samples.

(a) Analysis of peak currents versus scanning rates for Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD. (b) Evaluation of diffusion-controlled and capacitive contributions at scanning rates of 2, 5, 20, and 50 mV s−1, and (c) determination of the capacitive contribution ratio across different scanning rates for Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD. (d) Comparison of the capacitive currents at various scanning rates among FeNiCo-PBA, Fe-NiCoOP, and Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD.

(a) Illustrates the fundamental mechanism of a supercapacitor configured with a Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD-based cathode and an activated carbon (AC) anode. (b) Displays CV profiles at a scan rate of 2 mV s−1 for voltage compatibility assessment between the Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD and AC electrodes. (c) CV curves employed to establish the operational voltage range of the supercapacitor. (d, e) Exhibits comprehensive CV and GCD profiles of the assembled device. (f) Depicts the specific capacitance derived from the GCD curves.

(a) Ragone plots of the supercapacitor compared with the reported devices. (b) Cycling performance of the device for 8000 cycles at 10A g−1, the inset reflects the SEM image of Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD after cycling performance. (c) The photograph of LEDs lighted up by the device connected in series. (d, e) Self-discharge and leakage current analysis of Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD//AC.
Performance comparison with other FeCoNi composite based supercapacitors.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots modified polymetallic oxyphosphide hollow nanoboxes for boosting supercapacitor applications

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Physics and Electronic Information & Henan Key Laboratory of Electromagnetic Transformation and Detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots modified polymetallic oxyphosphide is rational synthesized with enhanced charge transfer kinetics and ion transport efficiency for high performance supercapacitor.

The regulation of composition and structural design of electrods are pivotal in advancing high-performance supercapacitors. In this study, we prepare a Fe-doped NiCo oxyphosphide (Fe-NiCoOP) nanobox with a hollow structure, which is further modified with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQD). This innovative design integrates multiple high-performance elements, leading to a substantial enhancement in electrochemical properties. The introduction of phosphorus (P) substantially improves the material’s conductivity, optimizes its electrochemical active sites, and increases cycle stability. The integration of NGQD forms a distinctive heterostructure with Fe-NiCoOP, which not only refines the charge transport pathways but also markedly accelerates the reaction kinetics of the electrode material. Furthermore, the hollow architecture of the nanocages provides extensive permeation and storage spaces for the electrolyte, while also substantially increasing the redox reaction sites, thereby elevating the overall electrochemical performance. The synergistic combination of these advantages results in the Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD electrode material exhibiting an exceptional specific capacity of 1136.5 F g−1. When utilized in supercapacitors, this material demonstrates a remarkable energy density of 78.7 Wh kg−1. Notably, the device retains 81.6% of its initial capacitance after 8000 charge–discharge cycles, underscoring its outstanding cyclic stability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitors have gained extensive utilization across diverse sectors such as renewable energy, transportation, and industrial applications [1,2]. Moreover, they are increasingly being incorporated into various components of modern power systems, including power generation, transmission and distribution networks, and energy management systems [3,4]. Despite their widespread adoption, supercapacitors face significant challenges, particularly the relatively low energy density of electrode materials and the tendency of these materials to detach during operation [5]. Electrode material is a critical factor in determining the capacitance of supercapacitors, as different materials exhibit varying specific surface areas and electrochemical activities, which directly impact the energy storage capacity of the device [6]. While transition metal oxides (TMOs) have emerged as promising candidates, their practical implementation faces fundamental limitations including restricted surface active sites, inadequate electrical conductivity, and structural instability during prolonged cycling [7,8]. Recent advances in ternary metal oxyphosphides have demonstrated enhanced electrochemical performance through phosphorus-induced electronic structure modulation and improved ion diffusion kinetics [9,10]. For instance, Chen et al. reported that CoZnNi oxyphosphide nanosheets achieved a remarkable specific capacitance of 2096.4 F cm−3 through phosphorus-mediated charge redistribution [11], while Wang's group developed P-Doped MnMoO4 Nanosheets showing 84.5 % capacity retention after 10,000 cycles [12].

Prussian blue analogs (PBAs) have recently attracted attention as potential supercapacitor electrodes due to their unique open-framework structure and tunable metal centers [13–15]. However, their inherent limitations in electrical conductivity and structural collapse during ion insertion/extraction processes have restricted practical applications [16,17]. Previous attempts to enhance PBA performance have focused primarily on single modification strategies-either structural engineering (e.g., hollowing or porous structures) [18], elemental doping [19], or conductive material hybridization [20]. For example, Zhu et al. improved NiCo PBA capacity by 377 % through hollow cubic structure fabrication and vulcanizing method [21], while Park's team achieved 116.67 % cyclic stability via graphene wrapping [22]. Nevertheless, these individual approaches fail to address the multifactorial limitations of PBAs comprehensively.

Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (NGQD) have emerged as a promising material for enhancing the electrochemical performance of graphene by modifying its local electronic structure through the incorporation of nitrogen atoms [23,24]. NGQD are characterized by their large specific surface area and abundant heteroatomic functional groups, which facilitate electrolyte penetration and enhance ion adsorption/desorption processes, thereby improving capacitive performance [25,26]. Furthermore, NGQD can be integrated with other high-performance electrode materials to form composite structures that leverage multi-component synergistic effects. These composites exhibit enhanced ion diffusion capabilities, leading to superior overall performance of the electrode materials [27,28]. For example, Xu et al. demonstrated the potential of such composites by developing a CuCo-LDH/N-GQDs electrode, which exhibited an impressive specific capacitance of 1009 F g−1 and achieved a maximum energy density of 58.6 Wh kg−1 when assembled into a supercapacitor with activated carbon [29].

This work introduces three critical innovations that overcome existing limitations in PBA-based supercapacitor electrodes: (1) The development of a ternary Fe-doped NiCo oxyphosphide system combining the high redox activity of Ni/Co with Fe-induced structural stabilization; (2) A hierarchical architecture featuring hollow cubic frameworks for enhanced electrolyte accessibility coupled with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot NGQD surface modification for superior charge transfer; (3) The strategic integration of phosphorus doping and NGQD hybridization to synergistically optimize both bulk and interfacial properties. This synergistic approach has not been previously reported for PBA-derived materials. Through this comprehensive material design, we achieve unprecedented electrochemical performance: The optimized Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD electrode delivers an exceptional specific capacity of 1136.5 F g−1 at 2 A g−1. When employed in supercapacitor devices, this material achieves a high energy density of 78.7 Wh kg−1. Remarkably, the device maintains 81.6 % of its initial capacitance after 8000 charge–discharge cycles, underscoring its exceptional cycling stability and potential for long-term energy storage applications.

Cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O), Nickel nitrate hexahydrate (Ni(NO3)2·6H2O), potassium hexacyanoferrate(III) (K3[Fe(CN)6]), NaH2PO2·H2O, sodium citrate pentahydrate (Na3C6H5O7·5H2O), ammonium citrate tribasic (C6H8O7·3H3N) and ethanol are purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.

In a standard process, a clear solution A is prepared by dissolving 0.75 mmol of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, 025 mmol of Co(NO3)2·6H2O, and 1.25 mmol of Na3C6H5O7·5H2O in 50 mL of deionized water. Subsequently, 0.5 mmol of K3[Fe(CN)6] is dissolved in another 50 mL of deionized water to form a clear solution B. Solution B is then carefully poured into solution A while stirring at room temperature. After maintaining continuous stirring for 10 min, the combined solution is allowed to stand at room temperature for 24 h. The resulting NiCoFe-PBA precipitates are harvested using centrifugation and thoroughly rinsed with both deionized water and ethanol in succession.

The Fe-NiCoOP composite was synthesized through a straightforward one-step high-temperature phosphating process. In this method, a mixture of NaH2PO2·H2O and NiCoFe-PBA, with a mass ratio of 3:1, was placed inside a double-zone tubular furnace. The mixture was then subjected to a reaction at 350 °C for a duration of 2 h in an argon-filled environment, resulting in the formation of the NiCoFe-OP composite. The synthesis procedure for N-GQD involves the following steps: Initially, 1 g of tribasic ammonium citrate was dissolved in 20 mL of deionized (DI) water and subsequently heated to 200 °C in an oil bath. After a duration of 30 min, the solution underwent a color change to a light orange hue. To neutralize the pH of the solution, an adequate quantity of sodium hydroxide solution (concentration of 10 mg/mL) was introduced. This led to the formation of NGQD. Subsequently, Fe-NiCoOP powder was dispersed in NGQD and stirred for 20 min, the resultant Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD composite was thoroughly washed with ethanol and water, followed by drying in an oven for a period of 12 h.

Electrochemical assessments of the Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD composite were conducted in a three-electrode configuration utilizing a 6 M KOH aqueous electrolyte solution with an electrochemical workstation (CHI 760E). A Pt electrode served as the counter electrode, while a Hg/HgO electrode was employed as the reference electrode. For the preparation of the working electrodes, a homogeneous mixture of the synthesized powder, acetylene black, and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) in a weight ratio of 8:1:1 was dispersed in N-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP) via ultrasonic treatment for 30 min. The resultant slurry was then coated onto a nickel foam substrate (1 cm × 1 cm) and dried under vacuum at 60 °C for a minimum of 12 h, achieving a typical active material loading of approximately 2.5 mg cm−2. Additionally, the electrochemical properties of a Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD//activated carbon (AC) supercapacitor were evaluated in a two-electrode setup. The electrochemical performance was characterized through cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) cycles, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements.

The formation process of the Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD composite material is illustrated in Fig. 1 . The synthesis begins with the careful preparation of FeNiCo-PBA nanocubes using a liquid-phase coprecipitation method, which acts as the initial precursor. Following this, at a phosphatization temperature of 350 °C, the NiFe-PBA decomposes and partially transforms into phosphide, resulting in the creation of hollow Fe-NiCoOP nanoboxes. In the final step, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQD) are attached to the surface of these nanoboxes through electrostatic adsorption, producing the Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD composite nanoboxes. This sophisticated synthesis approach guarantees accurate assembly and combination of the components, thereby enhancing the overall performance of the composite material.

The phase composition of the synthesized material was characterized using XRD. As illustrated in Fig. S1, the FeNiCo-PBA precursor exhibits a perfect match with the standard reference patterns of NiFe-PBA (PDF#51-1897) and CoFe-PBA (PDF#31-1000), confirming the successful synthesis of the precursor. Following high-temperature partial phosphating, diffraction peaks corresponding to CoP (PDF#89-4862) and NiCo2O4 (PDF#73-1702) are observed, indicating the successful conversion of FeNiCo-PBA into Fe-NiCoOP. Upon integration with NGQD, a broadened diffraction peak emerges at 20.2°, signifying the successful preparation of Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD. XPS was utilized to study the elemental composition, electronic structure, and oxidation states. The XPS survey spectra of Fe-NiCoOP and Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD (Fig. 3b) confirm the presence of Co, Ni, Fe, O, P, and C, with the N element in Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD originating from NGQD. High-resolution XPS spectra of Fe 2p, Ni 2p, Co 2p, P 2p, O 1s, and C 1s are presented in Fig. 3c–h, respectively. The XPS spectrum of Fe 2p, depicted in Fig. 2 c. The peaks at 707.0 eV and 709.8 eV belong to Fe2+ and Fe3+ (2p3/2), respectively, while the peaks at 719.7 eV and 720.2 eV correspond to Fe2+ and Fe3+ (2p1/2). After NGQD modification, the peak position of Fe2+ remained basically unchanged, while the peak position of Fe3+ shifted from the position with high binding energy, indicating that Fe atoms carry effective positive charges due to electron transfer, leading to an increase in their binding energy [30,31]. In the Ni 2p spectrum illustrated in Fig. 3 d, the binding energies positioned at 853.5 eV (2p3/2) and 870.9 eV (2p1/2) are indicative of Ni2+, whereas the peaks situated at 856.4 eV (2p3/2) and 873.7 eV (2p1/2) are representative of Ni3+, as referenced in [32]. When compared to Fe-NiCoOP, the slight decrement in binding energies observed hints at a robust interaction with NGQD, which could facilitate improved electron transfer within the electrode material. The Co 2p spectrum (Fig. 3e) reveals peaks associated with Co2+ and Co3+ states, similarly, after binding with NGQD, the binding energy slightly shifts towards lower energy, further confirming the chemical modifications induced by NGQD [33,34]. In the P 2p spectra (Fig. 3f), peaks at 132.2 eV and 135.4 eV are assigned to metal phosphide, while a peak at 139.8 eV arises from partial surface oxidation upon exposure to air [35]. O 1s spectra in Fig. 3g displays four distinct peaks: one at 529.1 is ascribed to M−O bonds, 530.4 eV is ascribed to PO bonds, another at 531.2 eV representing MOH bonds, and a fourth at 532.7 eV associated with HOH bonds [36]. From the Fig. S2, it can be seen that in NiCoFe-PBA, the peak intensity of M−O located at 529.0 eV decreases after P doping, indicating a change in the bonding environment of M−O after P doping. In addition, peaks of PO bonds in Fe-NiCoOP and Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD, indicating the possibility of P atoms replacing O atoms [37]. The C 1s spectrum depicted in Fig. 3h reveals three distinct peaks. One peak is located at 284.8 eV and is attributed to CC bonds, another at 286.0 eV arises from CO bonds, and a third peak, positioned at 288.8 eV, corresponds to O–CO bonds [38]. The N 1s spectrum of Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD (Fig. 3i) reveals peaks at 399.1 eV (pyridinic N), 400.1 eV (pyrrolic N), and 401.5 eV (graphitic N), confirming successful nitrogen doping from NGQD and highlighting its impact on the electronic properties of the composite [39]. The chemical composition and functional groups of the composite materials were analyzed by Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (FTIR) spectroscopy (Fig. S3a). The spectrum exhibits characteristic absorption bands corresponding to various functional groups: a broad peak at 3406 cm−1 is attributed to OH stretching vibrations of hydroxyl groups in nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQD), while the band at 1638 cm−1 arises from COOH bending/stretching vibrations, confirming the presence of carboxyl groups in NGQD [40]. Additionally, the absorption at 1053 cm−1 corresponds to CO bending vibrations of epoxy groups. A distinct peak at 563 cm−1 indicates the formation of metal–oxygen (M−O) bonds. Furthermore, the weak band observed at 2969 cm−1 can be assigned to CH stretching vibrations [41]. These FTIR results collectively demonstrate the successful incorporation of functional groups and the formation of chemical bonds in the synthesized composite material. Through Raman spectroscopy (Fig. S3b), we further investigated the chemical composition and structural features of Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD. The spectrum exhibits characteristic peaks at 193 cm−1, 545 cm−1 and 713 cm−1, corresponding to the vibrational modes of Co–O, Ni–O and Fe–O bonds, respectively, confirming the presence of metal–oxygen coordination in the composite [42,43]. Additionally, two prominent peaks appear at 1343 cm−1 (d-band) and 1595 cm−1 (G-band), which are typical Raman signatures of graphitic carbon materials. The d-band arises from structural defects and disordered sp3 carbon, while the G-band corresponds to the in-plane vibrational mode of sp2-hybridized carbon, indicating the presence of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQD) in the composite [43]. These Raman results, combined with FTIR analysis, provide compelling evidence for the successful integration of Fe-NiCoOP with NGQD, highlighting the hybrid material's well-defined chemical structure and bonding interactions.

The morphological features of the synthesized samples were investigated using SEM and TEM techniques. Fig. 3a displays the SEM image of the as-prepared FeNiCo-PBA precursor. The image reveals that the precursor adopts a regular hexahedral morphology, characterized by a smooth surface and a size of approximately 300 nm. Following the phosphatization process and subsequent composite formation with NGQD (Fig. 3b), the material retains its intact cubic structure. However, a noticeable granular texture appears on the surface, which may indicate the formation of metal oxides or phosphides. Observations from fractured regions further confirm its hollow interior structure. This preservation of the cubic shape alongside the surface modifications underscores the robustness of the synthesis approach and the intricate structural transformations undergone during the composite formation. The TEM image in Fig. 3c exhibits a hollow structure with a morphology featuring a diameter of approximately 300 nm, which is consistent with the SEM results. From the magnified TEM image (Fig. 3d) focusing on the edge of the hollow cage, it can be observed that its interior is comprised of fine nanoparticles. However, due to the minute size of the quantum dots, HRTEM was utilized to investigate their surface. The NGQD are marked with yellow circles in Fig. 3e. The interplanar spacing of 0.29 nm corresponds to the (220) plane of spinel NiCo2O4 but is larger than the theoretical interplanar spacing, suggesting that oxygen atoms have been replaced by phosphorus atoms after modification. This reveals that the widened interplanar spacing of the oxygen-phosphide layer facilitates the storage of hydroxyl ions. The darker lattice stripes with an interplanar spacing of 0.25 nm correspond to the (111) plane of CoP. Additionally, the lattice stripes with an interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm are consistent with the (100) plane of NGQD. Furthermore, the TEM image, accompanied by the corresponding elemental energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) scanning profiles depicted in Fig. 3f–m, along with the elemental mapping signals, collectively demonstrate a homogeneous distribution of cobalt, iron, nickel, oxygen, phosphorus, nitrogen and carbon across the entire structure. This observation aligns well with the XPS results, providing additional evidence for the successful formation of Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD composites. BET analysis revealed a high specific surface area (26.5 m2 g−1) and mesoporous structure (∼8.4 nm) in the composite (Fig. S4), promoting electrolyte accessibility and efficient mass transfer for electrochemical processes.

To assess the capacity performance of the electrode materials, cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) measurements were conducted using a conventional three-electrode system. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the CV curve of Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD encloses a larger area compared to those of Fe-NiCoOP and FeNiCo-PBA, demonstrating that the hybrid Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD electrode delivers superior capacity performance. The corresponding CV and GCD curves for these materials are provided in Supplementary Fig. S5. A comparative analysis of the GCD curves further confirms that the electrochemical capacity of the final Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD composite significantly outperforms that of Fe-NiCoOP, consistent with the CV results. Fig. 4c presents the CV curves of Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD at scan rates ranging from 2 to 100 mV s−1, which display well-defined and symmetric anodic and cathodic peaks. This indicates that the reversible Faradaic reactions of Fe-NiCoOP and NGQD facilitate rapid electron/ion transport and diffusion-controlled electrochemical processes. The underlying Faradaic reactions can be reasonably described as follows [44,45].
NiCo2O4 + OH–+H2O ↔ NiOOH + 2CoOOH + 2e-(1)
NiOOH + OH– ↔ NiO2 + H2O + e-(2)
CoOOH + OH– ↔ CoO2 + H2O + e-(3)
CoP + OH–↔ CoPOH + e- (4)
CoPOH + OH–↔ CoPO + H2O + e-(5)

The specific capacitance (C) of the Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD electrode was calculated using the formula C = (i × Δt)/m ΔV and shown in Fig. 4d, where i is the discharge current in amperes (A), Δt is the discharge time in seconds (s), ΔV is the potential window, and m is the mass loading of Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD on the nickel foam. The Qs values for Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD were found to be 1136.5 F g−1 at 2 A g−1 and 809.2 F g−1 at 10 A g−1. Remarkably, when the current density is increased from 2 A g−1 to 10 A g−1, the electrode maintained a high-capacity retention rate of 71.2 % (Fig. 4e), significantly outperforming FeNiCo-PBA (54.9 %) and Fe-NiCoOP (64.6 %). This underscores the synergistic benefits of combining Fe-NiCoOP with NGQD.

To further analyze the electrochemical performance of the hybrid electrode materials, Nyquist plots of Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD and Fe-NiCoOP were compared over a frequency range of 0.01–100 kHz (Fig. 4f). In these plots, the charge transfer resistance (Rct) is represented by the diameter of the semicircle at high frequencies, while the equivalent internal resistance (Rs) is determined by the intercept on the real axis [18]. The Constant Phase Element (CPE) accounts for non-ideal capacitive behavior, often caused by factors such as surface roughness, porosity, and uneven current distribution [46]. The impedance parameters derived from the fitting process are detailed in Table S1. The Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD electrode demonstrated a lower Rs value of 0.72 Ω compared to Fe-NiCoOP (0.83 Ω), indicating reduced contact resistance between the electrode and the electrolyte. Additionally, the Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD electrode exhibited a smaller semicircle radius (Rct) of 3.5 Ω, suggesting lower charge transfer resistance at the electrode–electrolyte interface. This implies that Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD facilitates faster ion diffusion and improved electrical conductivity. Moreover, the steeper slope of the linear regression line in the low-frequency region for Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD, compared to other electrodes, indicates lower electrolyte diffusion resistance, further highlighting its superior electrochemical performance.

The energy storage mechanism and reaction kinetics of Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD were examined through CV curves at scan rates ranging from 2 to 50 mV s−1. As illustrated in Fig. S6a, the CV curve exhibits distinct redox peaks and retains its shape as the scan rate escalates, suggesting the electrode material's favorable reversibility. Furthermore, the shifts of the oxidation peak to higher potentials and the reduction peak to lower potentials with increasing scan rates are attributed to Faraday reversible reactions and electrode polarization, respectively. Higher scan rates augment the internal diffusion resistance. The relationship between peak current (i) and scan rate (v) is defined as: [47].
i = avb (2)
where a and b are constants, and the value of b determines the nature of the energy storage mechanism. Specifically, b = 0.5 signifies an ion-diffusion-controlled process, whereas b = 1 represents a capacitive storage process. As depicted in Fig. 5 a, the calculated b values for the redox peaks of Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD are 0.6151 and 0.6022, respectively, indicating a combined ion diffusion and capacitive storage behavior.

To quantify the contributions of surface capacitance and diffusion control further, the linear relationship between i and v was analyzed using the equation: [48].
i = k1 v + k2 v 0.5(3)
where k1 v and k2 v 0.5 represent the surface capacitance and diffusion control processes, respectively. The fitted CV curves, presented in Fig. 5b and Fig. S6b, emphasize the surface capacitance contributions marked in green. Fig. 5c displays a bar chart illustrating these contributions, showing that as the scan rate increases, the surface capacitance contribution rises from 13.29 % to 41.01 %. This indicates that surface capacitance increasingly dominates at high scan rates due to shortened ion diffusion distances, which expedite electron transfer and enhance surface redox reactions. The electrochemical active surface area (ECSA), closely associated with the electrochemical double-layer capacitance (Cdl), is a pivotal indicator of the electrode material's electrochemical performance. Cdl values for Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD and Fe-NiCoOP were calculated from CV curves within a 0.23–0.33 V potential window (Fig. S5c–e). Notably, Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD exhibited the highest Cdl value of 19.6 mF cm-2, indicating a higher number of active sites and underscoring the significance of NGQD in enhancing the performance of Fe-NiCoOP electrode.

Fig. 6 a elucidates the fundamental charge storage mechanism within the fabricated hybrid supercapacitors, which employ Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD as the cathode material and activated carbon (AC) as the anode material, both immersed in a 6 M KOH aqueous electrolyte. The voltage operating ranges of Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD (0–0.5 V vs. Hg/HgO) and AC (−1–0 V vs. Hg/HgO, Fig. S7) were carefully optimized and harmonized to enable the efficient assembly of the asymmetric supercapacitor configuration, as evidenced by the cyclic voltammetry (CV) profiles recorded at a scan rate of 2 mV s−1 in Fig. 6b. To systematically determine the optimal operational voltage window for the hybrid supercapacitors, a comprehensive series of CV measurements were conducted at progressively increasing voltage values, as presented in Fig. 6c. The onset of significant polarization observed in the CV curve at 1.7 V clearly indicates that the stable electrochemical window for these devices is effectively limited to 0–1.6 V.

The electrochemical performance of the supercapacitor was further characterized through CV measurements across a wide range of scan rates from 2 to 100 mV s−1 within the optimized voltage window of 0–1.6 V, as shown in Fig. 6d. The remarkable retention of the characteristic CV curve shape, even at elevated scan rates, demonstrates the exceptional rate capability and fast charge–discharge characteristics of the device, which can be attributed to the efficient ion transport and charge transfer kinetics within the hybrid electrode architecture.

Quantitative evaluation of the supercapacitor's energy storage capacity was performed through galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) measurements, with the corresponding curves displayed in Fig. 6e. As quantitatively summarized in Fig. 6f, the device exhibited impressive gravimetric capacitances of 221.2, 183.6, 152.4, 117.2, 87.5, and 76.2 F g−1 at progressively increasing current densities of 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, and 10 A g−1, respectively. This performance metric, coupled with the excellent rate capability demonstrated in the CV measurements, highlights the superior electrochemical properties of the Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD//AC hybrid supercapacitor configuration, making it a promising candidate for high-performance energy storage applications.

The Ragone plot presented in Fig. 7 a showcases the relationship between energy density and power density. At a power density of 800 W kg−1, Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD//AC attains a peak energy density of 78.7 Wh kg−1. When compared to recently published supercapacitors characterized by their mass energy density in Table 1 , our device exhibits notable energy storage superiority. It is noteworthy that, following 8,000 cycles at a current density of 10 A g−1, the device maintains a capacity retention rate of 81.6 % and boasts a coulomb efficiency of 94.4 %, highlighting the exceptional stability of the electrode material under extended charge–discharge cycles and elevated current densities. The inset in Fig. 7b illustrates the morphological characteristics of the sample after prolonged charging and discharging, revealing that the Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD nanobox structure remains partially intact, but most of it exhibits minor collapse, which could contribute to the slight decline in performance. Furthermore, by connecting two devices in series, we demonstrate the practical application by lighting up seven blue-emitting LEDs for 60 s (Fig. 7c). To evaluate the durability and safety of supercapacitors assembled from experimentally prepared electrode materials, self-discharge and leakage current tests were conducted on Fe-NiCoOP//AC and Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD//AC devices. Fig. 7d shows the self-discharge curves obtained after charging the devices to 1.6 V at a current density of 0.5 A g-1 and then leaving them for 48 h. As shown in Fig. 7d, Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD//AC exhibits a smaller voltage drop compared to Fe-NiCoOP//AC, indicating a slower self-discharge rate. Moreover, Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD//AC retains 27.4 % of its voltage after 48 h. Fig. 7e presents the leakage current curves obtained from a 24-hour leakage current test. From the figure, it can be observed that the current of the devices rapidly decays initially and then gradually stabilizes. Eventually, the currents of Fe-NiCoOP//AC and Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD//AC stabilize at 0.9828 mA and 0.5515 mA, respectively, indicating that the Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD//AC device has a lower leakage current. Additionally, from the local magnification, it is evident that the Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD//AC device consistently exhibits a smaller leakage current than Fe-NiCoOP//AC at any given time. Based on the self-discharge and leakage current curves, the Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD//AC device demonstrates a slower self-discharge rate and a lower leakage current, suggesting that Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD//AC possesses a longer lifespan and higher safety.

In conclusion, we have pioneered the development of a novel Fe-NiCoOP nanocaged hollow structure electrode material, ingeniously modified with graphene quantum dots. The incorporation of phosphorus doping and the integration with graphene quantum dots have synergistically created a heterogeneous structure that significantly bolsters the material's electrical conductivity and refines the pathways for charge transport. Moreover, the nanobox's hollow architecture facilitates enhanced electrolyte infiltration and substantially increases the active sites available for redox reactions. The confluence of these benefits endows the Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD electrode material with an impressive capacity of 1136.5 F g−1. Furthermore, when deployed in supercapacitors, this electrode material demonstrates exceptional energy density and outstanding cyclic stability. Consequently, the Fe-NiCoOP@NGQD electrode materials, distinguished by their superior electrochemical performance, high energy density, and exceptional cycle stability, undoubtedly chart a novel course for the exploration and advancement of high-performance supercapacitors. Additionally, these materials infuse new energy into the evolution of energy storage technology materials, marking a significant leap forward in the realm of supercapacitor applications.

Yan Cao: Data curation, Conceptualization. Rong Zheng: Investigation, Formal analysis. Ketong Zhang: Project administration. Feng Chen: Project administration. Yingzi Wang: Supervision. Chenyang Cheng: Investigation. Yiwen He: Project administration. Hong Jia: Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Shihao Xiao: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision. Yu Liu: Writing – review & editing, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the significant science and technology projects of LongMen Laboratory in Henan Province (231100220100), the significant science and technology projects of LongMen Laboratory in Henan Province (231100221100), the Key research and development program in Natural Science Foundation of Henan (231111222200), the College Students Innovations Special Project (Grant Nos. 202510482012), the Key Scientific Research Projects of Higher Education Institutions in Henan Province (Grant No. 25A140012) and Nanjing/Jiangsu XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd. of China.
